questions.scenario: Your script has a repeated greeting block. Refactor it into a reusable function.
questions.question: Define a function called 'greet' that prints 'Hello, operator!' and call it.
questions.description: Functions in bash: function_name() { commands; }. No argument list in the definition — arguments are accessed via $1, $2, etc. Define before calling. Functions run in the current shell context (not a subshell).
use_cases: Avoid repeating the same commands multiple times
Group related operations under a meaningful name
Make scripts easier to read and maintain
hints: Call it by just writing the function name

questions.scenario: A function uses a counter variable. Prove that without 'local', it leaks into the caller's scope.
questions.question: Define a function that sets a variable with 'local'. Show the variable is empty outside the function.
questions.description: 'local var=value' limits a variable to the function's scope. Without local, all variables are global and persist after the function returns — a common source of bugs in larger scripts.
use_cases: Prevent function variables from polluting global scope
Write reusable functions that don't cause side effects
Safe counter and temp variables in recursive functions
hints: Run: f() { local RESULT='hidden'; echo "inside: $RESULT"; } && f && echo "outside: $RESULT"

validation.command: f() { local RESULT='hidden'; echo "inside: $RESULT"; } && f && echo "outside: '$RESULT'"

questions.scenario: A function checks if a service name is valid. It should communicate pass/fail via exit code, not a global variable.
questions.question: Write a function 'is_valid' that returns 0 if the argument matches ^[a-z]+$ and 1 otherwise. Test with 'nginx'.
questions.description: Functions use 'return N' to return an exit code (0-255). This is NOT like a language return value — you can't return strings. To return strings, use echo inside the function and capture with $().
use_cases: Validation functions that integrate with && / || logic
hints: return 0 for success, return 1 for failure
Run: is_valid() { [[ "$1" =~ ^[a-z]+$ ]] && return 0 || return 1; } && is_valid nginx && echo 'valid'

validation.command: is_valid() { [[ "$1" =~ ^[a-z]+$ ]] && return 0 || return 1; } && is_valid nginx && echo 'valid'

questions.scenario: A logging function must prefix every message with a timestamp and level.
questions.question: Write a log_msg function that takes LEVEL and MSG as arguments and prints: '[LEVEL] MSG'.
questions.description: Function arguments are accessed via $1, $2, $@. Assign them to local named variables immediately for clarity: local level=$1. $# inside a function gives the argument count for that function call.
use_cases: Consistent log format across a script
Wrapping complex logic behind simple interfaces
hints: Assign to local vars for readability
Run: log_msg() { local level=$1; local msg=$2; echo "[$level] $msg"; } && log_msg INFO 'System online'

validation.command: log_msg() { local level=$1; local msg=$2; echo "[$level] $msg"; } && log_msg INFO 'System online'

questions.scenario: A function needs to know its own name for a self-referential error message.
questions.question: Write a function that prints its own name using $FUNCNAME, then call it.
questions.description: $FUNCNAME is an array — $FUNCNAME[0] is the current function, $FUNCNAME[1] is the caller, and so on. Useful for error messages that report exactly which function failed, without hardcoding names.
use_cases: Debug call stacks in complex scripts
Avoid hardcoding function names in error strings
hints: $FUNCNAME inside a function gives its name
